hook: Blue-tiled Persian facade over Tbilisi's sulphur baths
facts: Mosaic-tiled minaret facade in the style of an 1840s bathhouse; rebuilt and reopened in 2017
Sits over the natural sulphur springs that gave the city its name (tbili, 'warm')
Pushkin and Dumas both bathed in Abanotubani during their travels
Domed brick bath chambers run underground beside it
legend: Legend says King Vakhtang Gorgasali's falcon downed a pheasant that fell into a hot spring here, revealing the waters and prompting him to found the city
w: The only bath in Abanotubani that flaunts itself: a blue-and-turquoise tiled facade with twin minarets, more Isfahan than Caucasus. Legend says a king's falcon downed a pheasant into a steaming spring here, and Tbilisi got both its springs and its name, tbili, warm. Pushkin soaked below.

hook: A 22-metre waterfall hidden in a gorge behind the baths
facts: Reached by a short canyon walk straight behind the sulphur bathhouses
Name means 'fig gorge' for the fig trees that once grew here
Fed by the Tsavkisistskali stream descending off the Mtatsminda ridge
Long a parking lot, the gorge was cleared and reopened to walkers in 2012
legend: Legend says Queen Tamar used the falls as her own private bath
w: Slip behind the domed baths, follow the cliff-walled gorge a few minutes, and the city falls away into a 22-metre cascade. Locals once washed laundry in its stream; the name means fig gorge, for trees long gone. They say Queen Tamar bathed here. Until 2012, only a car park.

hook: Georgia's writers buried on the holy mountain slope
facts: Necropolis around St David's church 'Mamadaviti', formalised in 1929
First burials: playwright Alexander Griboyedov and his Georgian wife Nino Chavchavadze
Griboyedov was killed by a mob at the Russian embassy in Tehran in 1829
Holds many of Georgia's foremost poets, painters and national figures
legend: Nino's epitaph for Griboyedov reads, in effect, 'why did your love outlive you?'
w: Cut into the holy mountain around a church, where Georgia buries its poets. The first grave belongs to Griboyedov, torn apart by a Tehran mob in 1829, and beside him his teenage widow Nino, who carved his stone with a line that still aches: why did your love outlive you. Mourning in marble.

hook: The oldest surviving church in the city
facts: Three-nave basilica dated to the 6th century, built under King Dachi of Iberia
Renamed in 1675 for the Ancha icon brought here to escape an Ottoman advance
Damaged and rebuilt repeatedly through Persian and Turkish wars
Returned to worship in 1991; known for its men's chant choir
w: Squat, plain, and around fifteen centuries old, this brick basilica went up when Tbilisi first became a capital. Its name comes from a treasured icon hauled here in 1675 to keep it from the Ottomans. Burned, sacked and patched through endless wars, it still hosts a chant choir whose drone predates the dome.

hook: A deliberately leaning fairy-tale tower of salvaged tiles
facts: Built in 2010 by puppeteer and artist Rezo Gabriadze beside his marionette theatre
Assembled from fragments of abandoned old-town buildings
An angel emerges hourly with a hammer to strike the bell
Twice daily a tiny puppet play, 'The Circle of Life', opens in the tower
w: It tilts on purpose. The puppeteer Rezo Gabriadze cobbled this tower together in 2010 from bits of dying old-town houses and hundreds of his own hand-painted tiles, a rebuke to glossy redevelopment. Every hour an angel pops out to ring the bell; twice a day a wordless puppet life plays out, birth to farewell.

hook: A Soviet sewing factory reborn as a creative yard
facts: Built in the Soviet era as the 'Nino' women's-clothing factory, then abandoned in 1991
Reopened in 2016 by Multiverse Architecture and the Adjara Group
Now a courtyard of bars, ateliers, a vinyl shop and the region's biggest hostel
One vintage store sells clothes salvaged from the original Nino factory
w: For 25 years the old Nino sewing factory stood gutted and silent in Chugureti. Since 2016 its concrete shell has filled with bars, studios, a record shop and beds for hundreds of backpackers, all opening onto a graffiti-walled yard. One stall still sells clothes salvaged from the dead factory's own floors.

hook: Sprawling food market named for army deserters
facts: Named for Red Army deserters who sold their kit here after the 1921 Soviet takeover
Grew up beside the central railway station as a flea market
Now the largest food market in the capital, spilling across a whole district
Mounds of churchkhela, spices, cheese, pickles and seasonal produce
w: In the early 1920s, Red Army men fleeing a lost war washed up by the railway station and sold whatever they carried, rifles included. The deserters vanished; their name stuck. A century on, this remains the city's biggest food market, all spice mounds, churchkhela ropes and barrels of brined everything.

hook: Flea market of Soviet relics on a riverless bridge
facts: Held on the 1849-51 Dry Bridge, left 'dry' when a river branch was filled in
Became an antiques market spontaneously in the hard 1990s
Stalls heaped with medals, ID books, cameras and propaganda kitsch
Now spreads into the adjoining Dedaena and 9 March parks
w: The bridge lost its river when an old channel was filled, so it spans dry land and a flea market instead. In the broke 1990s people came to sell their own belongings; now the rails groan with Soviet medals, Lenin busts, caviar tins and dead men's photographs. A whole vanished country, laid out for haggling.

hook: Aluminium giantess holding wine and a sword
facts: Twenty-metre figure raised on Sololaki hill in 1958 for the city's 1500th anniversary
Sculpted by Elguja Amashukeli, who privately called her simply 'Capital'
A wine bowl for friends in one hand, a sword for enemies in the other
The weathered original was swapped for an identical copy in 1997
w: She looms over the old town in national dress, a bowl of wine in her left hand for those who come as friends and a sword in her right for those who don't. The whole Georgian character in two props. Her sculptor called her, drily, Capital; everyone else made her the city's mother. The 1958 original now stands in copy.

hook: Tsereteli's colossal unfinished pillars above the Tbilisi Sea
facts: Sixteen bronze-clad pillars up to 35 metres tall, begun by Zurab Tsereteli in 1985
Upper bands carve kings and queens; lower bands tell the life of Christ
Stands on Keeni hill near the artificial Tbilisi Sea reservoir
Never formally completed, yet often dubbed Georgia's Stonehenge
w: Marooned on a bald hill above the reservoir, sixteen black pillars climb 35 metres: kings and queens carved up top, the life of Christ below. Tsereteli began them in 1985 and never quite finished, so they read as ruins of a monument that never opened. Tour buses skip it. The wind up here does not.

hook: A whole Georgia of timber houses rebuilt on a wooded hill
facts: Opened in 1966 on 52 hectares above Vake, near Turtle Lake
Around 70 vernacular buildings moved here from every region of Georgia
Arranged by ethnographic zone, from Svaneti towers to Kakhetian wine houses
Named for ethnographer Giorgi Chitaia, who founded it
w: Someone dismantled houses across all of Georgia, hauled the timbers to a hillside above the city, and stood them back up: Svan defensive towers, Kakhetian wine cellars, Khevsur stone homes, scattered through oak woods over 52 hectares. A country folded into one forest, where you walk from region to region in minutes.

hook: A 1905 cliff railway hauling up the holy mountain
facts: Opened in 1905 on the initiative of a Belgian tramway company
Climbs Mount Mtatsminda, one of the steepest funiculars anywhere
Stops mid-slope at St David's church and the writers' Pantheon
The upper station's restaurant has crowned the summit for decades
w: Belgian tram engineers dreamed of an upper city on the mountain and built this near-vertical railway in 1905 to reach it. The upper town never came; the funicular stayed, hauling people past the holy mountain's churches and graves to a summit restaurant. Halfway up, it pauses at the slope where the poets are buried.

hook: A faded Soviet pleasure ground on the city's summit
facts: Laid out by the Soviet government in the 1930s at 770 metres
Once ranked the third most-visited park in the entire USSR
A 65-metre Ferris wheel turns right at the cliff edge
Reachable by the 1905 funicular from the old town below
w: Up at 770 metres sits a Soviet fun park, once the third most-visited in the whole union, now half-melancholy carousels and dark rides under a vast TV tower. A Ferris wheel hangs its cars straight off the cliff lip, the entire city laid out below. Cotton candy and Brutalism, in the thinning air.

hook: Cobbled stairs climbing through old wooden Tbilisi
facts: Around 120 steps scaling Sololaki hill toward the Narikala ridge
Lined with carved wooden balconies and stained-glass verandas
Lower and Upper Betlemi churches sit along and atop the climb
Named for Bethlehem; long the locals' route between upper and lower town
w: Old Tbilisi as it was before the renovations reached it: a stone stair of some 120 steps switchbacking up Sololaki hill past leaning houses, fretworked balconies and two small churches. The route locals once climbed daily between low town and high. Cats, washing lines and a widening view at every turn.

hook: Old seat of the patriarchs, keeping Georgia's holiest relic
facts: Origins in the late 6th or early 7th century, beside the Mtkvari river
For over a thousand years the principal cathedral of the Georgian church
Holds the grapevine cross of St Nino, who converted the kingdom in the 4th century
Rebuilt many times after Persian and Mongol sackings
legend: Tradition says the Virgin gave St Nino a cross of vine branches, which she bound with her own hair
w: For a thousand years the patriarchs sat here, by the river that keeps sacking and rebuilding the city around them. Inside rests Georgia's holiest object: a cross of grapevine wood. Tradition says the Virgin handed it to St Nino, who lashed it together with her own braided hair before converting a kingdom.

hook: A glass wave of a bridge that blinks in Morse code
facts: Designed by Italian architect Michele De Lucchi, opened in 2010
156 metres of steel and glass with over 10,000 LEDs
Lights flash an hourly Morse message naming elements of the human body
Brought from Italy on some 200 trucks; locals nickname it accordingly
w: A 156-metre wave of glass and steel slung over the Mtkvari, shipped from Italy on 200 trucks. After dark its 10,000 lights pulse an hourly message in Morse, spelling out the chemical elements of the human body. Old-towners, less reverent, named it after a sanitary product. Both readings stick.

hook: Cellar bar of Georgia's natural qvevri winemakers
facts: Opened in 2012 as the country's first dedicated natural wine bar
Founded collectively by several leading artisan producers
Pours qvevri wines fermented in buried clay, a method some 8,000 years old
Set in an arched brick cellar in Sololaki
w: Down brick stairs in Sololaki, six of the country's small natural winemakers pooled together in 2012 to pour their own bottles. The wines here ferment in qvevri, egg-shaped clay jars buried in the earth, a technique some eight thousand years old. Amber, cloudy, faintly wild. The cradle of wine, served by the glass.

hook: City museum inside a rebuilt Silk Road caravanserai
facts: Housed in a 19th-century caravanserai near Sioni cathedral
Earlier inns on the site were levelled when Persia sacked Tbilisi in 1795
Founded as a city museum in 1910; around 50,000 objects
A wine museum now occupies hidden chambers beneath it
w: Merchants once stabled camels and slept around this courtyard on the road between empires; Persia burned the earlier inn to the ground in 1795. Rebuilt, the caravanserai now keeps the city's own memory, fifty thousand objects of daily Tiflis life, with a wine museum tucked into the cellars below the old yard.

hook: The world's first railway run by children
facts: Opened in 1935, recognised as the first children's railway anywhere
Built on a 1.2-km narrow-gauge loop in a 19th-century Persian-laid garden
Children aged 9-15 worked as conductors, signalmen and inspectors
An original 1930s locomotive still runs in summer
w: A Persian cleric laid out this garden in the 1830s; the Soviets gave it the world's very first children's railway in 1935. On a narrow-gauge loop, kids of nine to fifteen worked the signals, punched the tickets and drove the train, learning a trade by playing at one. A 1930s engine still runs each summer.

hook: A wild concrete wedding palace turned oligarch's home
facts: Completed in 1984 by architect Victor Djorbenadze as a Soviet wedding hall
Fuses 1920s expressionism with medieval Georgian church forms
Bought in 2002 by tycoon Badri Patarkatsishvili as a private residence
Now leased out for weddings and events; interiors echo a cathedral
w: Djorbenadze built it in 1984 so Soviet couples could marry somewhere grander than a registry desk, and produced something between a cathedral and a sci-fi cave, all soaring concrete and curved light. Then an oligarch bought it in 2002 to live in. Today it rents out for weddings again. Faith smuggled in as form.
